questions.stem: What is the genetic material of all living organisms?
questions.explanation: DNA is the genetic material of all living organisms; some viruses use RNA, but viruses are not considered living.

questions.scenario: A particular virus stores its genetic information as RNA.
questions.stem: What does this tell us about the virus?

options.text: It is not considered a living organism
It must be the ancestor of all cells

questions.explanation: Some viruses use RNA as their genetic material, but viruses are not considered to be living organisms.

questions.stem: Which three components make up a single nucleotide?

options.text: A phosphate, a pentose sugar and a nitrogenous base
An amino acid, a sugar and a lipid
A fatty acid, glycerol and a phosphate

questions.explanation: Each nucleotide is made of a phosphate group, a pentose (5-carbon) sugar and a nitrogenous base.

questions.scenario: In diagrams, a pentagon is often used to represent one part of a nucleotide.
questions.stem: Which component does the pentagon represent?
questions.explanation: By convention circles represent phosphates, pentagons represent pentose sugars and rectangles represent bases.

questions.stem: What forms the 'backbone' of a DNA or RNA strand?

options.text: Amino acids joined in a chain

questions.explanation: Sugar–phosphate bonding creates a continuous covalently bonded chain forming the strong backbone of each strand.

questions.stem: The bonds joining nucleotides along the sugar–phosphate backbone are:
questions.explanation: The backbone is a continuous chain of covalently bonded atoms, giving each strand strength.

questions.stem: Which four nitrogenous bases are found in DNA?
questions.explanation: DNA contains adenine, thymine, guanine and cytosine; RNA replaces thymine with uracil.

questions.stem: The sequence of bases along a strand of DNA is important because it:

options.text: Forms the basis of a genetic code

questions.explanation: The bases form the basis of a code, storing genetic information in their sequence.

questions.stem: RNA is a polymer formed by joining nucleotide monomers through which type of reaction?
questions.explanation: RNA is a polymer formed by condensation reactions that link nucleotide monomers, releasing water.

questions.stem: In DNA, adenine always pairs with:
questions.explanation: Complementary base pairing in DNA pairs adenine with thymine, and guanine with cytosine.

questions.stem: In DNA, guanine pairs with:
questions.explanation: Guanine pairs with cytosine through hydrogen bonding, one of the two complementary base pairs in DNA.

questions.stem: The two strands of a DNA molecule are described as antiparallel. This means they:
questions.explanation: The two DNA strands are antiparallel, running in opposite directions to each other.

questions.stem: The two strands of DNA are held together by:

options.text: Hydrogen bonds between complementary base pairs

questions.explanation: Hydrogen bonds between complementary base pairs link the two antiparallel strands of the double helix.

questions.stem: Which base is found in RNA but NOT in DNA?
questions.explanation: RNA contains uracil in place of the thymine found in DNA.

questions.stem: Which pentose sugar is found in DNA?
questions.explanation: DNA contains deoxyribose, while RNA contains ribose.

questions.stem: Which row correctly contrasts DNA and RNA?

options.text: DNA: double-stranded, deoxyribose, thymine; RNA: single-stranded, ribose, uracil
DNA: single-stranded, ribose, uracil; RNA: double-stranded, deoxyribose, thymine
Both are double-stranded and contain uracil

questions.explanation: DNA is double-stranded with deoxyribose and thymine; RNA is usually single-stranded with ribose and uracil.

questions.stem: Compared with DNA, a typical RNA molecule has:
questions.explanation: RNA is usually single-stranded, whereas DNA is double-stranded.

questions.stem: Complementary base pairing is essential because it allows genetic information to be:
questions.explanation: Complementary base pairing, based on hydrogen bonding, allows genetic information to be replicated and expressed.

questions.stem: What is the complementary base sequence?
questions.explanation: Applying A–T and G–C pairing, ATGC pairs with TACG.

questions.stem: Why does DNA have a limitless capacity for storing information?

options.text: Any length of molecule and any base sequence is possible
It contains an infinite number of sugars

questions.explanation: Because a DNA molecule can be of any length and have any base sequence, its capacity to store information is effectively limitless.

questions.stem: DNA is said to store information 'with great economy'. This refers to:

options.text: Storing enormous amounts of data in a very small molecule
Storing no more than four instructions

questions.explanation: DNA stores an enormous quantity of information in an extremely compact form, i.e. with great economy.

questions.scenario: The same genetic code is used by bacteria, plants and animals.
questions.stem: This conservation of the genetic code is evidence for:

options.text: Separate independent origins for each kingdom
That DNA is not the genetic material
That code changes randomly each generation

questions.explanation: The genetic code being conserved across all life forms is evidence of universal common ancestry.

questions.scenario: In a diagram, a circle represents part of a nucleotide.
questions.stem: Which component is the circle used for?
questions.explanation: By convention, circles represent phosphates, pentagons sugars, and rectangles bases.

questions.stem: The monomers that join by condensation to form RNA are:
questions.explanation: RNA is a polymer of nucleotide monomers joined by condensation.

questions.stem: What percentage of its bases are thymine?
questions.explanation: Because adenine pairs with thymine, their percentages are equal, so thymine is also 30%.

questions.scenario: A DNA molecule has 30% adenine and therefore 30% thymine.
questions.stem: What percentage of its bases are cytosine?
questions.explanation: A + T = 60%, leaving 40% for G + C; since G = C, cytosine is 20%.

questions.stem: Which statement about nucleic acids is correct?

options.text: All living organisms use DNA as their genetic material
All living organisms use RNA as their genetic material
Living organisms use protein as their genetic material
Living organisms have no genetic material

questions.explanation: DNA is the genetic material of all living organisms; only some non-living viruses use RNA.

questions.stem: Why is the sugar–phosphate backbone described as 'strong'?

options.text: It is a continuous chain of covalent bonds
It is held together only by weak hydrogen bonds

questions.explanation: The backbone's continuous covalent bonding makes it a strong structural framework for each strand.

questions.stem: Ribose differs from deoxyribose because ribose has:

options.text: One more oxygen atom (an extra –OH group)

questions.explanation: Ribose has an –OH group where deoxyribose has only –H, i.e. ribose has one extra oxygen.

questions.stem: The near-universal genetic code supports the idea that all life:
questions.explanation: Conservation of the genetic code across all life forms is evidence of universal common ancestry.

questions.stem: Nitrogenous bases are attached to which part of a nucleotide?
questions.explanation: The nitrogenous base is bonded to the pentose sugar, which is also bonded to the phosphate.

questions.scenario: Two organisms have DNA of the same length but different base sequences.
questions.stem: This is possible because:

options.text: Any base sequence is possible for a given length of DNA
DNA can only ever have one sequence
Base sequence does not vary between organisms

questions.explanation: Diversity comes from the fact that any base sequence is possible in a molecule of any length.

questions.stem: During condensation to build a nucleic acid polymer, what small molecule is released?
questions.explanation: Condensation reactions join monomers and release a molecule of water each time.

questions.stem: In a diagram of DNA structure, students are expected to draw the two strands as:

options.text: Antiparallel, with A–T and G–C base pairs
Parallel, with A–G and T–C pairs

questions.explanation: Students should draw two antiparallel strands with adenine paired to thymine and guanine paired to cytosine.

questions.stem: Which statement explains why viruses are not classed as living even though they contain nucleic acid?

options.text: They rely on host cells and are not independent self-sustaining units
They are larger than all cells
They use protein instead of nucleic acid

questions.explanation: Although viruses contain DNA or RNA, they are not living because they cannot carry out life processes without a host cell.

questions.stem: Complementary base pairing depends on which type of bond between bases?
questions.explanation: Complementarity is based on hydrogen bonding between the paired bases.

questions.stem: How many different nitrogenous bases are normally found in a DNA molecule?
questions.explanation: DNA uses four bases (A, T, G, C), whose sequence forms the genetic code.

questions.stem: A pentose sugar is one that contains how many carbon atoms?
questions.explanation: 'Pentose' means a five-carbon sugar, such as ribose or deoxyribose.

questions.stem: Which of the following is an example of a nucleic acid?
questions.explanation: Messenger RNA is a nucleic acid; glycogen is a carbohydrate, collagen a protein and triglyceride a lipid.

questions.stem: The vast diversity of possible DNA base sequences is important because it allows:

options.text: Storage of the huge variety of genetic information found in life
All organisms to have identical genomes

questions.explanation: The limitless number of possible sequences underlies the enormous diversity of genetic information across life.

questions.stem: The directionality of a DNA strand is described using which notation?
questions.explanation: Directionality of nucleic acids is described by 5' to 3' linkages in the sugar–phosphate backbone.

questions.stem: Why is the 5'-to-3' directionality of nucleic acids significant?

options.text: It matters for replication, transcription and translation
It determines the colour of DNA
It changes which sugar is used

questions.explanation: The 5' to 3' directionality is significant for the processes of replication, transcription and translation.

questions.stem: In the DNA double helix, each base pair consists of:

options.text: One purine bonded to one pyrimidine

questions.explanation: Purine-to-pyrimidine pairing (A–T and C–G) gives each pair equal length, keeping the helix a uniform width.

questions.stem: Why does the DNA helix have the same three-dimensional shape regardless of its base sequence?

options.text: A–T and C–G pairs are of equal length
The backbone changes for each sequence

questions.explanation: Because purine–pyrimidine pairs are equal in length, the helix keeps the same width and structure whatever the sequence.

questions.stem: A nucleosome consists of DNA wrapped around a core of how many histone proteins?
questions.explanation: A nucleosome is DNA wrapped around a core of eight histone proteins, with an additional histone attached to linker DNA.

questions.stem: In a nucleosome, the additional (ninth) histone protein is associated with:

options.text: The linker DNA, helping hold the core together

questions.explanation: An additional histone protein attached to linker DNA helps hold the core of eight histones together.

questions.scenario: The Hershey–Chase experiment used radioactive labels on bacteriophages.
questions.stem: What did this experiment provide evidence for?

options.text: That DNA, not protein, is the genetic material
That protein is the genetic material

questions.explanation: The Hershey–Chase experiment supported the conclusion that DNA is the genetic material passed into cells.

questions.stem: The Hershey–Chase experiment became possible largely because of which technological development?

options.text: The availability of radioisotopes as research tools
The invention of the light microscope
The sequencing of the human genome

questions.explanation: When radioisotopes became available, they made the labelling used in the Hershey–Chase experiment possible.

questions.scenario: Chargaff measured the amounts of bases in DNA from many species.
questions.stem: What pattern did Chargaff's data reveal?

options.text: The amount of purines equals the amount of pyrimidines (A=T, G=C)
All four bases are always present in equal amounts
There is no relationship between base amounts

questions.explanation: Chargaff found the amounts of pyrimidine and purine bases were equal (A=T and G=C) across diverse life forms.

questions.stem: Purine–pyrimidine pairing contributes to DNA helix stability by ensuring that:

options.text: The distance across the helix stays constant
The two strands run in the same direction
The bases are joined by covalent bonds
The helix width changes with each pair

questions.explanation: Pairing a larger purine with a smaller pyrimidine keeps every rung the same length, giving a stable, uniform helix.
